summary:
The Great Chinese Famine of 1959–1961 was the deadliest famine in human history, killing an estimated 15 to 55 million people, with scholarly estimates most commonly in the range of 30–45 million deaths. The famine was caused primarily by the disastrous policies of the [[event:2705|Great Leap Forward]], Mao Zedong's program to rapidly industrialize China and collectivize agriculture through a combination of coercive grain procurement, ideologically driven agricultural practices, and the suppression of accurate reporting about the disaster's scale.

The famine's causes were multiple and interacting. Agricultural collectivization disrupted traditional farming systems. Unrealistic grain production quotas led local officials to falsify statistics and submit grain that communities needed for their own survival. Mao's theoretical belief that deep plowing and close planting would dramatically increase yields — both practices that actually damaged crops — was implemented under pressure. Backyard steel furnaces diverted agricultural labor and produced useless metal at the cost of neglected harvests. Despite the famine, China continued to export grain to the Soviet Union and other countries to maintain international prestige.

Knowledge of the famine's true scale was suppressed within China for decades. The Communist Party officially acknowledged in 1981 that mistakes had been made but continued to attribute the disaster primarily to natural calamities and Soviet withdrawal of technical assistance rather than to policy failures. Scholarly reassessment of the historical record, particularly the work of historians including Frank Dikötter and Jasper Becker, has established the primary role of government policy. The famine effectively discredited the most extreme phases of Maoist economics and contributed to the factional conflicts within the party that preceded the [[event:2689|Cultural Revolution]].
